permanence_risk_type: Policy Non-compliance; Weak Governance and Enforcement; Limited Data Transparency; Poor Management and Monitoring; Capacity, Expertise, and Resource Gaps; Weak Political Will and Enforcement
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning:
68% achieved full compliance (score 1) and 32% achieved less than full compliance (score 0), DOC does not yet appear to have a published compliance monitoring and enforcement strategy, as our results show, compliance reporting and data management are inconsistent. Only seven of the 20 cases had written records of compliance monitoring,the monitoring files were held in the various local offices where the monitoring was done. Financial records pre-2003 were archived in an old finance system to which there was limited access, , two compensation payments were never paid because permissions officers did not communicate with finance staff. Permissions staff signed contracts, yet finance staff never sent invoices.

Of the 261 concessions searched, approximately 15% asked for compensation. Among that 15%, a sample of 28 compensation conditions from 20 concessions was assessed. Of those 28, 68% achieved full compliance (score 1) and 32% achieved less than full compliance (score 0). Despite expectations that compliance on public conservation land would be higher than on private land (Dasgupta et al. 2000), our finding of 68% compliance under the Conservation Act is statistically indistinguishable from Brown’s (2013) finding of 65% under the RMA. The most common form of compensation requested was additional weed or pest control beyond that which DOC already performs. Other requirements included compensation payments to DOC, environmental premiums (payments intended to reflect the environmental impact of the concession activity on the land), planting, and species management programmes (i.e. management and monitoring of a particular species). Many of the compensation cases involved the exchange of dissimilar ecological values, such as weed control in exchange for the removal of habitat

study_title: Comprehensive review of carbon quantification by improved forest management offset protocols
program_name: California ARB Offset Program; American Carbon Registry (ACR); Climate Action Reserve (CAR); Verra VCS
policy_note: NA
policy_jurisdiction_note: NA
policy_description: NA
permanence_risk_category: Climate and Environmental Disturbances; Systemic Oversights and Risk Management Gaps
permanence_risk_type: Fire; Buffer Pool Shortfalls
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning:
Project terms are highly variable across protocols, but even the longest term (100 years) does not constitute a truly permanent offset equivalent to reducing fossil fuel emissions. Forest credits used to offset fossil fuel emissions convert carbon permanently stored as fossil fuels into carbon stored in trees in the short-term carbon cycle. If the end of a project term represents a reversal event, then non-permanent carbon storage (like all IFM projects) can more accurately be understood as delaying, not fully neutralizing, emissions (Herzog et al., 2003). Decisions about the appropriate duration of carbon storage fundamentally depend on assumptions about the future, and academics have called the default choice of 100 years “political” (Archer et al., 2009; Allen et al., 2016). In practice, project terms in IFM projects can range from 1 to 100 years, and there is not yet a widely adopted framework for comparing these different terms. Even taking for granted that these projects do not represent permanent offsets, questions remain about whether the current approach (relying on buffer pools) can achieve the promised durability.
Three key limitations of buffer pools could critically undermine their usefulness. First, none of the reviewed protocols take climate change into account in estimating buffer pool allocations and so may not reflect increasing risks of reversal over decadal time scales. For example, the ARB protocol for U.S.-based projects includes a buffer allocation of 2–4% for fire, 3% for biotic risks, and 3% for “other episodic catastrophic events” (e.g., drought). However, because annual acreage of forest fires in the United States is projected to quadruple by the end of the century even under a moderate emissions scenario (Anderegg et al., 2022), current buffer pool allocations may prove insufficient on the basis of wildfire risk alone. If recent wildfire trends continue in the United States, the entirety of the buffer pool for existing ARB projects will be consumed well before its intended lifetime is up (Badgley et al., 2022a). The ACR and VCS protocols have similarly low buffer allocations for natural disturbances, although no systematic assessment of these buffer pools have been conducted in the academic literature. A proposed VCS risk calculation tool may remedy this by using Climatic Impact Drivers (CIDs) to project increased risk.2

Second, some registries may not have a sufficiently diversified offset portfolio to effectively mitigate risk through the buffer pool mechanism. Such systemic risks may arise when a large proportion of projects in a registry are similar and/or exist in a constrained geographic area or ecological type. For example, the ARB compliance offset pool, which is composed mostly of IFM projects entirely in the United States (Badgley et al., 2022b), may be exposed to systemic forest risks that decrease the efficacy of the buffer pool as a risk mitigation tool.

Third, a buffer pool is defined by the quality of its constituent credits. Buffer pools composed of low quality credits have little value. Extensive work has shown systematic issues with additionality, baselines, leakage, and carbon accounting for land-based offset projects across protocols (e.g., Haya, 2019; West et al., 2020; Badgley et al., 2022b). Further, the ACR protocol allows project developers to put credits into the buffer pool from any ACR project (not just the project under consideration), which creates a perverse incentive to fill the buffer pool with low-value, potentially non-additional credits.

study_title: Conservation after the fact: The prevalence of post-approval condition-setting in environmental impact assessment processes in Australia and its implications for achieving ecologically sustainable development outcomes
policy_name: Australia EPBC Act; Australia EPBC Offsets Policy
policy_note: Statutory law passed by a legislature.; Non-binding guidance or strategy.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies to a single country at federal level.
policy_description: Primary environmental legislation in Australia; includes provisions for biodiversity conservation and offsets under specific approvals.; Outlines how environmental offsets are implemented under the EPBC Act.
permanence_risk_category: Compliance, Legal, and Governance Risks
permanence_risk_type: Inadequate Policy Design
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning: The results of this study demonstrate that “process shift” post-approval condition-setting for offsets is a prevalent practice under the EPBC Act. The presence of post-approval conditioning, in 48% of all offsets in the 2011–17 period and 54% of offsets in the 2021 sample, illuminates the government's high reliance on such condition-setting in practice. Furthermore, there is notable consistency between the two study periods. These results challenge the speculation in EIA literature that governments may be motivated to streamline processes to stimulate economic growth during economic downturns (Morgan, 2012; Bond et al., 2014), as high rates of post-approval condition-setting occurred across a decade of uninterrupted economic growth in Australia. Streamlining EIA processes through the use of post-approval condition-setting may be a product of bureaucratic budget constraints. However, this study was conducted over a period where the Department experienced both high funding levels (in 2011) and significant budget cuts (in 2014–15) (see Morton, 2017), without significant differences in the rates of post-approval condition-setting. Rather, the consistency of the results, provides some preliminary evidence that post-approval condition-setting is an entrenched part of administrative practice under the EPBC Act. Whilst further research is needed into the causes of streamlining and post-approval condition-setting, this study indicates that potential contributing factors include ‘cognitive biases’ or ‘internal group learnings’ within EIA administration (Plous, 1993: 109–111).

study_title: Conserve or convert? Pan-tropical modeling of REDD-bioenergy competition
policy_note: Overarching legislation or policy providing foundational principles.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies globally or through international institutions.
policy_description: Developed under the UNFCCC to incentivize emissions reductions through avoided deforestation and forest degradation; includes safeguards and provisions for biodiversity co-benefits.
permanence_risk_category: Socioeconomic and Equity Risks
permanence_risk_type: Incentive Misalignment, Opportunity Costs, and Landowner Behaviour
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning:
Fig. 1 and Table 2 display the extent and distribution of tropical evergreen forest with a positive NPV for the two biofuel crops and across regions. For the majority of the pan-tropical forest area—close to 60%—carbon pricing through, e.g., REDD+, does suffice to make deforestation unprofitable. However, for over 40% of the total pan-tropical evergreen forest area, the NPV of forest clearing for bioenergy plantations is positive even in the face of a carbon price on (avoided) emissions from deforestation; that is, bioenergy revenues more than offset the carbon cost of clearing (or foregone REDD+ earnings).

That climate policy induced demand for bioenergy can have a huge impact on future land use and land use change is already becoming evident. Here it has been shown that pricing the carbon emissions from deforestation—in the short term through an international REDD+ regime, in the longer run through tropical forest nations entering a climate regime with binding emission targets—can significantly alter the incentives for forest conservation vs. forest conversion to bioenergy plantations in the tropics. Still, for a substantial share of remaining evergreen tropical forests, carbon pricing alone does not suffice to make clearing unprofitable. Before discussing the implications of these results for conservation policy, other factors affecting the outcome of this analysis are deliberated upon.

study_title: Cost shifting and other perverse incentives in biodiversity offsetting in India
policy_name: India Forest Conservation Act; India Compensatory Afforestation Fund Act
policy_note: Statutory law passed by a legislature.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies to a single country at federal level.
policy_description: Requires central approval for non-forest use of forest land and mandates compensatory afforestation as an offset mechanism.; Establishes a national fund and authority (CAMPA) to manage compensatory afforestation and net present value payments for diverted forest land, supporting afforestation, biodiversity conservation, and ecological restoration.
permanence_risk_category: Compliance, Legal, and Governance Risks; Financial Risks; Socioeconomic and Equity Risks
permanence_risk_type: Weak Governance and Enforcement; Financial Mismanagement and Failure; Land Tenure and Access Conflicts; Community Exclusion and Compensation Gaps
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning:
Before 2002 the compensation funds collected from the user agencies were deposited directly with state governments, but in view of insufficient use of the funds and inadequate compensatory afforestation being carried out by the states, in 2002 the Supreme Court ordered (in the landmark case T.N. Godavarman Thirumalpad v. Union of India, 1995) the funds to be deposited in a centralized pool called the Compensatory Afforestation Fund (CAF). It also ordered the creation of a national-level authority called the Compensatory Funds Management and Planning Authority (CAMPA) for managing the funds and disbursing them to states. Upon receiving the funds, state governments (i.e., state forest departments) are expected to carry out the compensatory afforestation activity within 1 year or 2 growing seasons (CAG 2013).

Despite court orders, the central government failed to create CAMPA, even as funds kept accumulating in the CAF. In 2006, the apex court ordered the creation of an interim authority called the ad hoc CAMPA, with which the funds are presently held (Sridhar 2011; CAF Act 2016). A 2013 audit report of the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG), India's apex auditor of government expenditure, detailed how due to its “provisional nature” the ad hoc CAMPA had failed to effectively oversee seamless use of compensation funds and reemphasized the need for “a permanent, independent authority to provide guidelines and direction” (CAG 2013:14). The report also noted that compensation funds have been misused by the states for nonpermissible items such as administrative expenditure, building and maintenance of infrastructure, and purchase of vehicles and fuel (CAG 2013).

With its aim to increase forest cover by 5 million ha and improve quality of forest cover on another 5 million ha (MOEF 2011), GIM is expected to cost INR46,000 crores (approximately US$6.9 billion) over 10 years. Launched in 2011, the GIM did not commence until 2014 due to a lack of funds (Firstpost 2013). At the inception of the mission, it was envisaged that the funds for its implementation (in addition to the planned budgetary outlay) would be mobilized through convergence with related greening and restoration schemes of MOEF and of other ministries, such as the Ministry of Rural development and the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy. In absence of a formal funding structure, funds from other schemes could not be sourced for the first 3 years (Firstpost 2013; MOEF 2014b). In 2014, an outlay of INR13,000 crores (about US$2 billion) was approved for 2014 to 2018. Only a fraction of this amount is to be met from new funding (planned budgetary outlay). Ad hoc CAMPA has been stipulated as one of the schemes from which the remaining funds would be mobilized. In an apparent case of cost shifting facilitated by the provisions of the CAF Act, INR6,000 crores (about US$900 million) held by CAMPA are stipulated to be diverted to the GIM (MOEF 2014b; CAF Act 2016).

The implementation of compensatory afforestation is also resulting in adverse socioeconomic impact on local communities in some parts of India. According to reports, due to paucity of non-forest areas available for afforestation, forest departments are carrying out compensatory afforestation on land on which forest-dwelling tribes have rights (either claimed or settled) under the Forest Rights Act (FRA) of 2006 (Agarwal & Kukreti 2017). The FCA allows compensatory afforestation to be carried out on zudpi jungle, chhote, bade jhar ka jungle, jungle-jhari land, and civil soyam (MOEF 2004) (a provision reiterated in ministry guidelines issued in November 2017 [MOEFCC 2017]), which are multiuse forest areas historically managed by forest-dwelling communities but classified as non-forest (Kohli & Menon 2017). In contrast, the FRA recognizes the community and individual rights of forest-dwelling communities (referred to as scheduled tribes and other forest dwellers) to access, manage, and govern such areas and prohibits, for example, planting or mining in such areas until these rights have been settled. Once the rights are settled, FRA requires the consent of the village council (gram sabha) before any activity is carried out. However, the CAF Act has bypassed the requirement of consent for carrying out compensatory afforestation (Shrivastava 2017). Forest departments are forcibly carrying out compensatory afforestation in areas where claims have been settled and titles have been issued and in areas where rights are yet to be settled (Agarwal & Kukreti 2017). Once compensatory afforestation is completed, the area is registered as reserved forest and brought under the jurisdiction of the state forest department, thus completely dispossessing traditional dwellers of their rights (Agarwal & Kukreti 2017).

study_title: Creating Temporary Pools as Wetland Mitigation: How Well Do They Function?
ecosystem_type: temporary freshwater pool; vernal pool; ephemeral pond; seasonal pond; woodland pond
project_type: wetland creation; wetland restoration; temporary pool creation
policy_note: NA
policy_jurisdiction_note: NA
policy_description: NA
permanence_risk_category: Ecological Design and Implementation Failures; Climate and Environmental Disturbances
permanence_risk_type: Poor Ecological Design; Invasive Species, Insects, and Pathogens
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning:
Created pools were significantly smaller (326 ± 86 m2) than reference pools (588 ± 79 m2; Table 1). Created and reference pools did not differ in depth, area to depth ratio, or areal inputs of leaf litter (Table 1). Before leaf out, created pools received significantly more light than reference pools (Table 1). The trend was similar, but not significant, once leaves emerged on the trees (Table 1). Across the 2-year study, five of the seven created pools exhibited seasonal drying whereas all six reference pools dried.

Substrate type was similar among created and reference pools: the substrate was predominantly leaf litter, with some woody debris, and low amounts of rock and bare substrate (Table 2). Submerged aquatic plant and shrub cover were low and did not differ between created and reference pools (Table 2). Created pools had significantly more floating duckweed (Lemna sp.) than reference pools (Table 2). Additionally, while total emergent vegetation cover was similar among created and reference pools, the species differed. Both cattail (Typha latifolia and Typha angustifolia x latifolia) and common reed (Phragmites australis) cover were greater in created pools than reference pools, where neither species occurred (Table 2).Creating temporary pools to mitigate for destruction is common across northern and eastern North America. Yet the recipe for successfully creating temporary pools is far from complete, and our results have several important implications for improving pool construction. First, canopy cover and forest connectivity need to be considered during pool creation as they affect pool temperatures, food resources, and species occupancy. Secondly, we need to improve our ability to design pools with intermittent hydroperiods, which likely requires more research to better predict pool hydrology. Lastly, monitoring of created pools should be long-term (>3 years) and assess the physical, chemical, and biological parameters of pools and their interactions. While our study may be limited by the geographic distance among reference and created pools (~150 km), we believe the key findings of our study are broadly applicable to the temporary pools of glaciated northeastern North America. Furthermore, our findings provide clear directions for future studies to address the mechanisms underlying our observed patterns. Unraveling the causal mechanisms behind these trends will advance our knowledge and potential success in conserving these important resources. Overall, given our current knowledge, the ability of created pools to replicate the physical, chemical, and biological characteristics of reference pools is questionable.

study_title: Crediting peatland rewetting for carbon farming: some considerations amidst optimism
policy_note: NA
policy_jurisdiction_note: NA
policy_description: NA
permanence_risk_category: Ecological Design and Implementation Failures
permanence_risk_type: Implementation Failures
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning:
Water table drawdown during or after a rewetting project displaces GHG emissions into the future, meaning that rewetting projects that at some stage fail provide no climate benefit in the long run. This has important repercussions for the creditability of peatland projects and underpins that the value of peatland rewetting as a mitigation instrument strongly depends on successful and permanent implementation of a high water table.

Evaluating the atmospheric effect of different rewetting successes that take place over the first century but over much longer time scales gives a different picture: Over centuries, the atmospheric GHG load from all scenarios except for permanent rewetting will converge (Fig. 6). The convergence occurs because of the finite size of any peat deposit which implies a decrease of CO2 in the atmosphere after exhaustion of the source. This means that temporary rewetting represents a displaced emission without climate benefit in the long run. Table S1 details the corresponding difference in cumulative forcing. Whereas over 100 years of computation the duration of successful rewetting in the first century makes a difference, the benefit diminishes over 500 and 1000 years because of the onset of CO2 emission after water table drawdown until the peat deposit is fully oxidized.

study_title: Data transparency regarding the implementation of European ‘no net loss’ biodiversity policies
policy_name: EU Habitats Directive; France Biodiversity Law; Germany Nature Conservation Act; Netherlands Nature Protection Act; Sweden Environmental Code
policy_note: EU legal instrument binding member states to objectives.; Statutory law passed by a legislature.; Codified set of laws or regulations.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies across multiple countries within a region.; Applies to a single country at federal level.
policy_description: Requires Member States to ensure conservation of habitats and species; includes obligation for compensatory measures (offsets) under Article 6.4.; Codified offsetting rules and strengthened the mitigation hierarchy in France.; Established the Eingriffsregelung system, requiring avoidance, minimization, and ecological compensation (offsets) for significant impacts on nature and landscape.; Implemented aspects of the EU Habitats Directive, including provisions requiring compensation for impacts on protected areas; relevant precursor to biodiversity offset practice and no net loss policies in the Netherlands.; Comprehensive environmental legislation consolidating multiple acts, including provisions for ecological compensation and the application of the mitigation hierarchy.
permanence_risk_category: Data, Transparency, and Capacity Issues
permanence_risk_type: Limited Data Transparency
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning: For all four countries we studied, comprehensive information on offset projects is not yet systematically collated, digitised and disseminated on a national scale; and cannot be accessed remotely. There would likely be resource costs associated with improving offset data transparency. However, a conceptual pre-requisite for offsets is quantitative demonstration to stakeholders that biodiversity losses and gains associated with a development are balanced (BBOP (Business and Biodiversity Offsets Programme), 2012, Bull et al., 2013). Consequently, the cost burden of monitoring is no argument for non-transparency. While other European countries have implemented some offsets (e.g. Spain, UK), these four countries are considered leading practitioners in Europe for offset implementation (Tucker et al., 2014). Comprehensive assessment of these four nations alone thus likely captures a substantial proportion of all implemented offsets in Europe.

study_title: Demand for Stream Mitigation in Colorado, USA
project_type: stream restoration; bank stabilization; off-channel reservoir construction
program_name: US CWA 404 Permitting; US Mitigation Banking
policy_note: Statutory law passed by a legislature.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies to a single country at federal level.
policy_description: Section 404 governs permitting for wetland and stream impacts.
permanence_risk_category: Direct Anthropogenic Disturbances; Ecological Design and Implementation Failures
permanence_risk_type: Landscape-level Pressures; Implementation Failures
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning:
Related to the water rights obstacle, everyone felt that there is not enough surface water available to maintain the environmental flows necessary to mimic the Natural Flow Regime [16] in order to ensure long-term success of the stream restoration projects. The state-level interviewees that were familiar with stream mitigation banking expressed their disappointment in the five-year success criteria for mitigation banks. One scenario that was mentioned was if riparian restoration (for stream mitigation) was performed on a Colorado River tributary where tamarisk is removed and riparian willows are planted in their place, after five years the willows would probably still be there and restoration would be considered successful. However, without long-term maintenance, tamarisk would likely invade soon after and return the fluvial system to its pre-restored state. The hope of this same individual is that stream mitigation banking would take a longer-term approach similar to conservation banking where an endowment is set up to ensure a successful restoration in perpetuity. Based on discussions with USACE representatives, it is possible (and has been put into at least one bank enabling instrument) to require these types of endowments for compensatory stream mitigation, particularly if invasive species removal is part of the restoration. USACE representatives stressed that project permanence is a requirement for mitigation bank approval. As for the five-year monitoring period for full release of mitigation credits, this can be negotiated; however, USACE is sensitive to the fact that longer monitoring periods would discourage mitigation banks, which is their preferred method of mitigation.

Another concern is the broad adoption of standardized approaches to stream restoration (e.g., Rosgen-based methods; [17]) rather than site-specific, process-based approaches. Those familiar with the Stream Quantification Tool (SQT) also expressed concern that this functional assessment tool that will be used to calculate stream impacts and credits relies too heavily on the Rosgen stream classification. Related to this concern, several interviewees felt that Colorado state agencies have prioritized engineering approaches to stream restoration over geomorphic and ecological approaches; and they fear that planned stream mitigation will reinforce and maybe even expand the engineering approach because it is easier. One interviewee pointed out that Bureau of Land Management (BLM) has been using more passive approaches to stream restoration (e.g., beaver dam analogues), and they hope that these passive approaches will be just as acceptable for stream mitigation as the more active engineering approaches that use heavy machinery and intensive earth-moving.

study_title: Disparity in biodiversity offset regulation across Australia may reduce effectiveness
subnational_region: Queensland; New South Wales; Victoria; South Australia; Western Australia; Tasmania; Australian Capital Territory
policy_name: Australia EPBC Act; Queensland Environmental Offsets Act; WA Environmental Offsets Policy; ACT Environmental Offsets Policy; SA Native Vegetation Act; Tasmania Forest Practices Act; Victoria Planning and Environment Act
policy_note: Statutory law passed by a legislature.; Non-binding guidance or strategy.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies to a single country at federal level.; Applies to states, provinces, or regions.
policy_description: Primary environmental legislation in Australia; includes provisions for biodiversity conservation and offsets under specific approvals.; Provides legal framework for biodiversity and environmental offsets in Queensland.; Guides use of offsets under WA Environmental Protection Act.; Guides offset requirements for environmental impacts in the Australian Capital Territory.; Governs clearing and offsetting of native vegetation in South Australia.; Regulates forest management and includes provisions for biodiversity protection in Tasmania.; Framework legislation for planning and development, including biodiversity considerations.
permanence_risk_category: Data, Transparency, and Capacity Issues; Compliance, Legal, and Governance Risks
permanence_risk_type: Limited Data Transparency; Inadequate Policy Design; Poor Management and Monitoring
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning:
All jurisdictions reviewed had legislation or policy that ensured some level of environmental transparency (Figure 1). Western Australia (WA) and Queensland had the greatest level of transparency, with matters related to biodiversity offsets clearly defined, as was an appeal process for developers and/or community. New South Wales (NSW), South Australia (SA), Victoria and the Commonwealth had clearly defined environmental biodiversity offset inclusions and processes. Tasmania and the Australian Capital Territory (ACT) had the lowest environmental transparency ratings as environmental biodiversity offset inclusions and processes were only partially defined.

All jurisdictions were found to have gaps in their ability to ensure transparency, measurability and enforceability for environmental, social and economic aspects of biodiversity offsets. This indicates that there is uncertainty with regard to biodiversity offsets in Australia being effective compensation measures for the impacts of development. Queensland had the best ratings for transparency and enforceability, being the only jurisdiction with dedicated biodiversity offset legislation. Other jurisdictions rely on conditions of approval following some form of environmental impact assessment (EIA) to impose offsets. An EIA is used to identify the likely environmental impacts of a development, and typically includes environmental studies as well as some form of stakeholder engagement (Bigard, Pioch, and Thompson Citation2017). In this way, the environmental and social impacts of a development are considered. In Australia, biodiversity offsets are then triggered when the EIA identifies that the development will have, or has the potential to have, a substantial negative impact on a particular species or habitat of concern despite mitigations (DSEWPaC Citation2012).

Transparency of matters to be offset also requires adequate consideration of the feasibility of siting offsets, especially when like-for-like biodiversity offsets are required. For example, a search of the NT’s NR Maps online application (DENR Citation2018) finds there are very few areas available for biodiversity offset development in the NT due to land tenure. This situation has arisen because the NT’s Territory Parks and Wildlife Conservation Act 2014 does not have the power to supersede legal determinations made in relation to land tenure made by other acts, including the Commonwealth’s Native Title Act 1993, or the NTs’ Mineral Titles Act 2010, Petroleum Act 2009 or Petroleum (Submerged Lands) Act 1967. These acts prevail over environmental legislation, meaning that if biodiversity offsets are created on land designated under these acts, then the biodiversity offset may be subject to development at any time in the future, affecting the potential for offsets to achieve adequate environmental outcomes. This leaves very few areas within the NT where biodiversity offsets could be sited. Accordingly, like-for-like biodiversity offsets on a large scale are unlikely to be possible in the NT, which should be factored into Commonwealth considerations of development applications in the NT. Land tenure issues are also likely to exist in other states and territories because the Commonwealth Native Title Act 1993 cannot be superseded anywhere in Australia, and mining and exploration legislation may also permit leases on land in which biodiversity offsets have been established. This has not been factored into biodiversity offsets legislation and policy in a transparent way, potentially resulting in developers being granted biodiversity offset conditions for developments that cannot be practically implemented. This issue might not be discovered until after environmental impacts have occurred, meaning that loss of biodiversity will occur (Bigard, Pioch, and Thompson Citation2017; Bull et al. Citation2013; Burgin Citation2008; Maron et al. Citation2010; Maron et al. Citation2012). Availability of land for biodiversity offset measures should therefore be a component of biodiversity offset design and should be considered by regulators when setting biodiversity offset conditions.

Queensland was the only jurisdiction that included environmental, social and economic aspects of biodiversity offsets in legislation. The ACT included environmental and social aspects of biodiversity offsets in statutory policy, and SA included economic aspects of biodiversity offsets in legislation. Otherwise, jurisdictions’ legislation and policy either did not specify aspects of biodiversity offsets, or it was included in non-statutory policies. This is a concern as there is now a legal precedent that Australian jurisdictions are not bound to implement non-statutory policies. In Western Australia, the Roe 8 development was announced in 2014, and in 2015 the ‘Save Beeliar Wetlands’ community group took legal action against the government claiming that it did not follow its own environmental policies when approving this development (Save Beeliar Wetlands (Inc) v Jacob [2015] WASC 482). This legal action was initially successful in December 2015, but was unanimously overturned on appeal in July 2016 on the grounds that the EPA’s policies were not ‘mandatory relevant considerations’ (Jacob v Save Beeliar Wetlands Inc [2016] WASCA 126). Despite this, if matters to be offset are not captured in statutory documents, the ability of regulators to enforce biodiversity offsets is unlikely to be restricted, as requirements may be enforced through obligation to meet conditions and many jurisdictions have legislation that enables their officers to take action should a condition not be complied with. It does mean, however, that there may again be an issue in terms of transparency and equity. This suggests that Australia is not consistently meeting its commitment to other nations under the CBD.

study_title: Do biodiversity offsets achieve No Net Loss? An evaluation of offsets in a French department
project_type: habitat maintenance; habitat management; wetland restoration; river restoration; pond creation; micro-habitat creation; linear plantation creation
policy_note: Statutory law passed by a legislature.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies to a single country at federal level.
policy_description: Codified offsetting rules and strengthened the mitigation hierarchy in France.
permanence_risk_category: Data, Transparency, and Capacity Issues
permanence_risk_type: Limited Data Transparency
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning:
Out of the 91 offset procedure files reviewed, only 33% contained the minimum information necessary to include the offsets in our analysis. Among the 30 projects selected, eight had to be excluded because the offset site manager did not respond to our inquiry. Finally, 22 procedures files were included in the analysis (see Table 2 for details on the projects). Since between one and five offsets were implemented on one or two sites per project (Table 2), a total of 59 offsets were surveyed. Nonetheless, information on the offset sites' initial state (both in quality and quantity) was absent from all 22 projects selected. Therefore, we were not able to verify whether actual gains were provided by the offsets. Only offset failure (absence of the target species and habitat on the offset site after offsets were implemented) could be analysed. In addition, some data expected to be found due to regulatory requirements were absent. Indeed, for seven out of 22 projects (33.9% of the offsets surveyed) the impacted site area was not found in the files. For six out 22 projects (23.7% of the offsets surveyed), no mention of a management plan was found in the files (even though an offset manager was in charge of the offsets).

We observed further that 25% of the procedure files did not contain a management plan, which we interpreted as an indication that no monitoring (and therefore no control) was foreseen by the developer. However, to reach NNL, international recommendations (BBOP, 2012) and the national legislation (law on biodiversity no. 2016-1087) stipulate that offset sites have to be maintained and managed by the developer until the gains are effective.

Second, the data found in the procedure files was of heterogeneous quality (i.e. missing data, confusion between minimisation and offsets), also noted for another department in France (Bigard et al., 2017). This restrained our analysis to potentially 33% of all offsets (assuming 100% were accessible). Moreover, given that we depended on the managers for access to the offset sites, our data set did not necessarily reflect the reality of the offset practices as there is a higher probability that managers did only grant access to the sites if they were confident in the success of their offset. Consequently, our results presented here might underestimate offset failure.
Even for projects that had been surveyed, available information was not sufficient to verify if the offsets provided the expected gains. The initial state of the offset site is the essential data missing, even in its simplest form: presence or absence of the target habitats and species. This information could be used in future analysis to measure gains of the target biodiversity features. These gains would be provided if the biodiversity features were absent at the offset site at the start of implementation but were present during the survey. To achieve NNL, quantitative criteria related to the dynamics of populations and counterfactual scenarios should also be measured (Bull et al., 2014). Other combinations such as no changes of the biodiversity features on the offset site, or even losses if they disappear from the site (e.g. meadow succession towards forest) could be considered as failures to achieve NNL.

study_title: Do habitat compensation schemes to offset losses from sea level rise and coastal squeeze represent a robust climate change adaptation response?
ecosystem_type: intertidal wetland; coastal wetland; saltmarsh; mudflat; transitional brackish habitat; freshwater grazing marsh
project_type: managed realignment; unmanaged realignment; tidal regulation; habitat creation; habitat restoration
policy_name: EU Habitats Directive; UK Habitats and Species Regulations
policy_note: EU legal instrument binding member states to objectives.; Binding rules issued under legislation.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies across multiple countries within a region.; Applies to a single country at federal level.
policy_description: Requires Member States to ensure conservation of habitats and species; includes obligation for compensatory measures (offsets) under Article 6.4.; Implements EU Habitats Directive in UK law; includes compensatory measures when damage to protected sites cannot be avoided.
permanence_risk_category: Data, Transparency, and Capacity Issues; Ecological Design and Implementation Failures; Climate and Environmental Disturbances
permanence_risk_type: Poor Management and Monitoring; Limited Data Transparency; Poor Ecological Design; Sea-level Rise; Flooding
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning:
Long-term monitoring is crucial for understanding habitat compensation issues but strongly reliant on data quality. Regular surveying and mapping of the intertidal zone at national scales has proved challenging, especially for countries such as the UK with an extensive coastline and frequent poor weather, combined with limited availability of aircraft or other data sources, although potentially improved through higher-resolution satellite data (Fitton et al., 2020). A key issue for intertidal habitats is the absence of fixed boundaries and difficulty in capturing habitats at their lowest tidal extent. Regarding compensation obligations, difficulties occur in distinguishing historic habitat losses from more recent changes, notably changes since the notional 1994 baseline used in England (Oaten et al., 2018). Monitoring has also largely concentrated on individual compensation sites, rather than N2K areas as a whole, which limits full evaluation of impacts, and although compensation sites are usually in close proximity to areas of habitat loss, this is not always the case (Morris et al., 2016).

Habitat changes may be attributable to sea-level rise, or other causes, either through coastal processes or direct climate effects, and further confounded by lag effects and shorter-term cycles. The 18.6-year lunar nodal cycle has an important influence on local water levels and hence intertidal habitat extents, especially in estuaries, and monitoring needs to take account of this cycle (Oaten et al., 2018). Populations of waterbird species that support site designations often undergo considerable inter-annual variations in response to both local and extraneous factors, hence using these to interpret trends in habitat availability is also very difficult.

Inadequate data can lead to inconsistencies in interpretation and progress reporting. For the two estuaries shared between national administrations of England and Wales (Dee and Severn), different interpretations of change have arisen (Miles and Richardson, 2018). In Essex and south Suffolk, annual saltmarsh loss associated with SMP policies was adjusted from 44 ha/year to <1 ha/year in a recent assessment (Environment Agency, 2018), potentially undermining confidence in the assessment process. Evaluation of UK coastal data quality (ONS, 2016) has identified a series of problems related to: (i) wide variation in methodologies, habitat classifications, and definitions; (ii) severe data limitations on physical extents, except for some protected areas; (iii) outdated biodiversity reporting data for many habitats. For habitat compensation schemes, these data limitations have important implications not only for interpreting current trends against changing drivers (sea-level rise etc.) but also confidence in projecting future trends and the required compensation response. Limitations of monitoring data have also been suggested to be a factor in constraining interpretation and acceptance of MR schemes, including their role in flood/erosion protection (Esteves and Williams, 2017).

An intertidal zone constrained by coastal defence structures therefore typically experiences changing abiotic properties detrimental to existing vegetation communities and becomes vulnerable to degradation and erosion (Timmerman et al., 2021). Vulnerability is also exacerbated because loss of intertidal vegetation also acts to reduce vegetative buffering of wave energy (e.g., Möller et al., 2014), and the coastal zone becomes increasingly at risk of erosion and flooding during extreme events (Zhang et al., 2021). In consequence, the intertidal zone becomes narrower as its low-water limit migrates landwards whilst the high-water limit remains fixed at the coastal defence.

study_title: Ecological compensation in spatial planning in Italy
ecosystem_type: woodland; river band; prairie; terrace; greenfield; agricultural land
policy_name: EU Habitats Directive; Italy Decree 357/1997; Italy Forestry Decree 227/2001
policy_note: EU legal instrument binding member states to objectives.; Legally binding executive order or administrative rule.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies across multiple countries within a region.; Applies to a single country at federal level.
policy_description: Requires Member States to ensure conservation of habitats and species; includes obligation for compensatory measures (offsets) under Article 6.4.; Transposes the EU Habitats Directive; requires compensatory measures for adverse impacts on Natura 2000 sites.; Promotes sustainable forest management and incentives for afforestation; indirectly supports ecological compensation in planning.
permanence_risk_category: Compliance, Legal, and Governance Risks; Data, Transparency, and Capacity Issues
permanence_risk_type: Inadequate Policy Design; Weak Governance and Enforcement; Poor Management and Monitoring
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning:
Unlike other European countries, no legal framework on ecological compensations at the planning level is in place in Italy, with two exceptions: (a) offsets of impacts affecting Natura 2000 areas, as this is required by European Directive 92/43/EC, transposed by national Decree no. 357/1997; and (b) reforestation in case of woodland clearance (National Decree no. 227/2001). Only in six cases were offset ratios specified, with requirements varying greatly: Plan no. 19 established that a 1:1 ratio of offset area must be guaranteed for all impacts affecting agricultural areas, regional parks and protected areas. Ratios of 1:1 and 1:2 were required by plan nos 25 and 27, respectively. Plan no. 16 established a basic offset ratio of 1:10, increased to 1.5:10 for specific high-value areas. Alternatively, municipalities can use a more detailed ‘Land Biodiversity Index’ based on a score system associated with land covers as a metric to calculate compensation. Plan no. 9 required a 1:1 ratio for land take by new development or, alternatively, 1 linear km of trees planted for each hectare of soil sealed. Plan no. 20 required offsets to be realized within the new development perimeter: at least 30% of the new residential developments area had to be kept greenfield; Plan no. 32 did not establish specific ratios but explicitly required in-kind offsets whenever possible. Partial enforcement mechanisms were established only by plan nos 13, 15, 16, 19 and 27, requiring that an offset realization schedule be detailed in the masterplans of new developments that municipalities had to elaborate and send to the Province for approval. In no case, however, were specific control mechanisms established to verify ex-post the actual realization and maintenance of compensation actions. Linked to this, only three plans (nos 16, 19 and 32) provided indicators aimed at monitoring offsets' implementation in the environmental monitoring programme. More detailed methodological guidance on how to realize offsets (containing examples and best practice) were found only in four cases (16, 17, 25 and 32).

Methodological and operational/administrative issues were poorly or not addressed by the majority of examined plans. Even in the most interesting cases, guidance provided to determine the kind and size of offsets was far from the level of detail used in other contexts (see for instance the German ‘Eingriffsregelung’ – Impact Mitigation Regulation; Wende et al. Citation2005; Darbi and Tausch Citation2010). Lack of clear and shared methods will probably hinder the diffusion of examples and case studies which, moreover, would be difficult to compare and translate to other planning contexts, even within the same Region, if the requirements of Provincial Plans differ. The findings of the empirical investigation conducted seems to point to two main critical elements:

1. Lack of legal requirements – current national and regional laws on planning and environmental assessment procedures do not provide for binding rules linking the possibility of new development with the obligation to realize compensation measures. The establishment of a clear legal framework thus appears to be the most critical point in the Italian context. Developers are not used to considering compensationas an integral element in their projects and they will continue to ignore this unless legally obliged to do so.

2. Lack of established methodologies – there is clearly an need for sound, but ‘ready-to-use’ methods and metrics to establish the amount and kind of compensation needed for different impacts. Although a straightforward methodology could be criticized on scientific grounds and be improved with practice, the lack of any established methods, which is the current situation, is the worst scenario.

study_title: Ecological compensation: An evaluation of regulatory compliance in New Zealand
project_type: habitat creation; habitat restoration; habitat enhancement; planting; pest control
policy_note: Statutory law passed by a legislature.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies to a single country at federal level.
policy_description: Framework for land and environmental planning.
permanence_risk_category: Compliance, Legal, and Governance Risks; Data, Transparency, and Capacity Issues
permanence_risk_type: Policy Non-compliance; Poor Management and Monitoring; Limited Data Transparency; Weak Governance and Enforcement
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning: The level of overall compliance with conditions was 64.8%, meaning that in two-thirds of cases the condition's requirements were met satisfactorily. The first is that the monitoring conditions were only met 63.6% of the time. The second is that the monitoring conditions were of varying quality and detail, from requiring a written confirmation of works having met conditions at the time of assessment, through to detailed and long-term monitoring of water quality with appropriate feedback loops. Finally, there was evidence of reports having been submitted but not necessarily being acknowledged or acted upon by the agency, which may diminish the incentive to comply if there is a perception of a lack of oversight. Random selection was not feasible for several reasons: There is no central national repository of consent information. There is rarely any recording of compensation requirements in council filing systems. Information collection and consent administration processes are highly variable across councils. Authors unable to randomly select data because: There is no central national repository of consent information. There is rarely any recording of compensation requirements in council filing systems. Information collection and consent administration processes are highly variable across councils. For example, Borrie et al.  argued that practice in New Zealand was lacking in comparison to other jurisdictions owing to policy ambivalence, implementation and enforcement issues and the lack of sufficient security measures available to ensure gains are realised and protected, noting: "we are profoundly concerned about this situation because it is already leading to the cumulative loss of New Zealand's valued biophysical environments."

study_title: Effectiveness of compensatory wetland mitigation in Massachusetts, USA
ecosystem_type: forested wetland; scrub/shrub wetland; wet meadow; marsh; freshwater wetland
project_type: wetland replication; wetland construction; wetland creation
policy_note: Statutory law passed by a legislature.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies to states, provinces, or regions.
policy_description: Provides state-level protection of wetlands in Massachusetts, requiring permits and mitigation for activities that may alter wetland areas.
permanence_risk_category: Compliance, Legal, and Governance Risks; Data, Transparency, and Capacity Issues; Ecological Design and Implementation Failures
permanence_risk_type: Policy Non-compliance; Poor Management and Monitoring; Limited Data Transparency; Poor Ecological Design
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning:
The majority of projects (54.4%) were not in compliance with the Massachusetts wetland regulations for a variety of reasons, including no attempt to build the project (21.9%), insufficient size or hydrology (29.8%), or insufficient cover of wetland plants (2.6%). Many of the projects constructed (64.9%) were smaller than required. The majority of constructed projects involved impacts to forested wetlands (71.1%). Most replication projects were designed to produce scrub/shrub systems (61.4%), but projects actually built produced no wetland (38.6%), wet meadows (36.8%), or some other wetland type (24.5%). The plant communities produced at replication sites differed significantly from the wetlands they were designed to replace in terms of number of species, cover, and species composition

Certificates of Compliance were on file for 109 (34%) of the 319 replication projects identified. Of the 109 COCs, 25 (23%) were issued before two growing seasons had elapsed, and 13 (12%) were issued within only one year of the Order of Conditions. The majority (54%) of replication sites in the study failed to meet the regulatory requirements of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts (Table 2). The largest single cause of failure (21.9%) was that no replication site had been built even though the OCs called for wetland replication.  Many sites also failed to meet the requirements because they were too small. Replication sites were considered too small to comply with the regulations if they were at least 10% smaller than required in the OC. Additional causes of failure included sites that did not support wetland vegetation because they were too dry, sites that were both too small and too dry, and sites where wetland vegetation was sparser than the 75% cover required in the regulations. Sites where the permitted construction project was never built, so that no impact actually occurred even though a permit had been issued, are reported in the ‘‘No Wetland Impact’’ category (Table 2).

The majority (65.8%) of replication projects did not have a Certificate of Compliance, despite the fact that adequate time had passed for the project to be completed and to have the two growing seasons necessary to determine compliance. In addition, two projects that were never constructed had a COC on file. Many COCs (22.9%) were issued before adequate time had elapsed in which to determine the long-term success of the project. The lack of a COC indicates that Conservation Commissions either had not inspected the project or that the project was inspected and found not to meet the requirements in the OC. However, only one file contained a record of a site being inspected and not meeting the requirements. This suggests that many projects may not have been inspected by the local CCs or that inadequate records of inspections are being kept. Without systematic inspection, Commissions cannot determine if replication efforts are successful at meeting regulatory requirements.

Many failed wetland replication projects were actually stormwater detention basins. These routinely failed to create wetland, either because they were too dry or too wet. Dry detention basins cannot support wetland vegetation. Flooded detention basins may provide some ephemeral aquatic habitat but not wetland habitat. If the design of the development requires stormwater detention, it should be designed separately.

study_title: Effects of section 404 permitting on freshwater wetlands in Louisiana, Alabama, and Mississippi
ecosystem_type: salt marsh; lacustrine bottom; lacustrine shore; palustrine aquatic bed; freshwater marsh; forested wetland; shrub wetland; pond; palustrine shore; lower riverine bottom
project_type: wetland creation; wetland restoration; wetland preservation
policy_note: Statutory law passed by a legislature.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies to a single country at federal level.
policy_description: Section 404 governs permitting for wetland and stream impacts.
permanence_risk_category: Compliance, Legal, and Governance Risks; Data, Transparency, and Capacity Issues
permanence_risk_type: Weak Governance and Enforcement; Poor Management and Monitoring; Limited Data Transparency
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning: Only information contained in the permit record was compiled and analyzed. No judgments were made concerning compliance with the terms of the permits or whether the compensatory wetland replaced the ecological functions of the wetland destroyed. Neither could be determined from the permit record. Permits requiring wetland compensation resulted in the net loss of 290.8 hectares. Over half of the impacted (59%) and compensatory (63%) wetlands were less than or equal to 4 hectares in size, and about 5% were greater than 40 hectares (Figure 2). The largest wetland impacted and the largest compensatory wetland were both 161.9 hectares. A large loss in area was associated with a small percentage of the permits. Of the 85 compensatory permits with complete area information, 81% had equal areas impacted and compensated. Twelve percent of the permits had larger areas impacted than compensated and resulted in a net loss of 198.2 hectares; 7% had larger areas compensated than impacted and resulted in a net gain of 30.4 hectares. The Section 404 permitting program could be evaluated more effectively if record-keeping was standardized. The results of this study demonstrate the utility of historic permitting information in evaluating a management program. However, the accuracy and thoroughness of this report was affected by poor record-keeping and difficulties encountered in data collection. The development of a computerized database would allow easy access to permit-related information. Forty-one percent of the permits issued in Louisiana required compensatory mitigation, but only 10% of those permits were monitored by at least one site visit. Information on the number &compensatory wetlands that were actually completed is not available because only 3% of the compensatory wetlands had a completion date listed.  Specific objectives for the mitigation projects were not documented. Only 10% of the compensatory wetlands were monitored by at least one site visit. In Louisiana, only eight percent of the total area impacted during the period examined was compensated. Possible reasons are that impacts to wetlands were either avoided and/or reduced and that some loss was accepted without requirements for compensation, In some cases, the COE did not require compensatory mitigation when other agencies recommended it, nor did they incorporate the recommendations of other resource agencies into the permit specifications.  In a survey of 266 permits issued by the COE, they found that only 46% of the recommendations made by NMFS were accepted by the COE. They also found that although compensatory mitigation was not required by the COE, habitat restoration often resulted from negotiations between permit applicants and the NMFS biologists.

study_title: Environmental integrity of forest offsets in a changing climate: embedding future climate in Australia's sinks policy regime
policy_note: Statutory law passed by a legislature.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies to a single country at federal level.
policy_description: Established the legal framework for issuing Australian Carbon Credit Units (ACCUs) for eligible emissions reduction and sequestration projects, including biodiversity co-benefits.
permanence_risk_category: Climate and Environmental Disturbances; Data, Transparency, and Capacity Issues
permanence_risk_type: Climate Change; Fire; Extreme Weather and Force Majeure Events; Drought; Capacity, Expertise, and Resource Gaps
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning: The vulnerability of particular forest types to the variability and extreme weather events that characterise Australia’s climate, and the capacity of those forests to lose carbon stocks in such events, also have a long research history. Drought is well understood as a cause of tree die-off; Australian studies in diverse regions have recorded drought-induced forest dieback mortality rates of 18–30% (Matusick et al. Citation2013; Silcock, Witt, and Fensham Citation2016; Fensham, Laffineur, and Allen Citation2019). High temperatures and heatwaves have been implicated in canopy, stem and plantation mortality in more temperate and higher rainfall regions (Butt, Pollock, and McAlpine Citation2013; Matusick et al. Citation2013; Ruthrof et al. Citation2018; Roxburgh, Paul, and Pinkard Citation2020). High intensity wildfires can also reduce the regenerative capacity of forests and increase the likelihood of future severe fires in southern Australia (Fairman, Bennett, and Nitschke Citation2019; Collins et al. Citation2021). Climate change projections reveal that extreme weather events could occur more often, and several studies project declining forest carbon sequestration rates that may exceed or challenge ERF risk management mechanisms (CSIRO and BoM Citation2015; Hobbs et al. Citation2016; Gordon et al. Citation2018; Roxburgh, Paul, and Pinkard Citation2020). Mitchell et al. (Citation2014) found droughts capable of causing significant tree mortality could increase from 1 in 24 years to 1 in 15 years by 2050, accompanied by a doubling of associated heat waves. With semi-arid woodlands already experiencing temperatures up to 46 °C, warming in coming decades is highly likely to exceed known thermal tolerance ranges (48 to 54 °C) and increase mortality rates (Curtis et al. Citation2014; Nolan et al. Citation2019). Climate change is now also challenging the assumption that post-fire regrowth will fully re-sequester carbon as the severe fire events of the 2019–2020 summer burnt traditionally wet forests that lack epicormic resprouting capacities, and shorter inter-fire intervals can increase the risk of woody plant extinction in regions likely to become hotter and drier (Enright et al. Citation2015; Roxburgh, Paul, and Pinkard Citation2020). Contributing to this weak interface, several participants stated that technical capacities regarding evaluation and incorporation of scientific evidence in policy and ERF methods have declined in the national government (Ind1,3; Sci1). The agencyFootnote1 responsible for the ERF was perceived by several participants as having a huge turnover of staff, and not enough ongoing knowledge (Ind2; Sci2). Consequent weaknesses in audit processes, including in feedback to methods development, were identified by participants across regime domains (Ind3; Sci3; Gov3). Further, research and industry participants identified that Government-provided tools are also starting to lose currency: “the last FullCAM update by the Government was suboptimal, based on poor consultation and not drawing on all the best data” (Ind2,3).

study_title: Estimating the financial risks of Andropogon gayanus to greenhouse gas abatement projects in northern Australia
policy_note: Statutory law passed by a legislature.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies to a single country at federal level.
policy_description: Established the legal framework for issuing Australian Carbon Credit Units (ACCUs) for eligible emissions reduction and sequestration projects, including biodiversity co-benefits.
permanence_risk_category: Climate and Environmental Disturbances
permanence_risk_type: Fire; Invasive Species, Insects, and Pathogens
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning: While Australia's savanna is largely intact, invasive grasses pose a major threat to its ecological function and biodiversity, particularly through increased fuel loads and changed fire regimes (Setterfield et al 2010, Brooks et al 2010, Foxcroft et al 2010). This threat is recognized in the CFI savanna burning methodology which lists invasive grasses with high biomass as a 'specific exception' (DCCEE 2011) to the vegetation types that can be included and therefore invaded land must be excluded from enrolment. The African grass Andropogon gayanus Kunth. (gamba grass) is the invasive grass species that poses the greatest threat to Australian savannas (Brooks et al 2010). Fine fuel loads in gamba grass invaded sites are three times higher than native grass sites and fire intensity increases significantly, from typically 1 to 3 MW m−1 in native grass fires to 16 MW m−1 in gamba grass fuelled fires in the early dry season (Setterfield et al 2010). Setterfield et al (2013) estimate that fuel loads in the heavily invaded region of the Northern Territory have increased from 6 to 10 t ha−1 due to gamba grass. The flame height is greater in the invaded sites resulting in more fire damage to the tree and shrub layers and reduction in the carbon stored in the woody component of the vegetation (Brooks et al 2010, Setterfield et al 2010). If invaded sites are not burnt in one fire season, the fire fuel load increases resulting in more intense, high combustion fires in the following year (Setterfield et al 2010).

study_title: Evaluating Restoration Success of Frequently Implemented Compensation Measures: Results and Demands for Control Procedures
ecosystem_type: oligotrophic dry grassland; mesotrophic meadow; mesotrophic pasture; mesotrophic wetland; dwarf shrub heath; tall herb field; hedge; brook; waterbody
project_type: grassland restoration; habitat enhancement; pond creation; brook revitalization; habitat establishment; habitat restoration
policy_note: Statutory law passed by a legislature.
policy_jurisdiction_note: Applies to a single country at federal level.
policy_description: Established the Eingriffsregelung system, requiring avoidance, minimization, and ecological compensation (offsets) for significant impacts on nature and landscape.
permanence_risk_category: Compliance, Legal, and Governance Risks; Data, Transparency, and Capacity Issues
permanence_risk_type: Policy Non-compliance; Limited Data Transparency; Poor Management and Monitoring; Weak Governance and Enforcement
permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning:
The examination of compensation plans, their implementation, and current management strategy revealed deficiencies regarding all these issues for 119 compensation sites in the remaining 31 areas (Table 5). Planning mistakes with regard to site conditions, implementation measures, and management instructions were identified particularly frequently. We found an incorrect assessment of the development potential caused by inadequate research or poor estimation of abiotic or biotic site conditions for 29% of the compensation sites. This often applied to plans aimed at the restoration of oligotrophic dry grasslands (8 of 20 sites, 40%).

A first inspection of planning documents and compensation areas revealed that 26 compensation areas had to be excluded from further evaluations (Fig. 1a) either because of insufficient goal setting with respect to habitat functions damaged by infrastructural development and/or poor descriptions of the measures (16), unrecognizable implementation (7), or because the measures were simply not carried out (3). Compensation projects were planned and implemented more than 15 years ago in 24 of these excluded areas.

Moreover, a recurrent problem was the lack of spatially and temporally specific management schemes for all biotopes that needed maintenance work. Instructions in compensation plans were primarily too vague or missing, and sometimes incorrect. This often led to deficiencies in development or maintenance management after implementation of measures. Here we found all kinds of combinations concerning deficiencies in management instructions and realized site management, which led to both suitable and unsuitable current management. Management instructions were vague or missing and current site management was unsuitable, or management instructions were vague or missing, but current site management was suitable, or management instructions were right, but current site management was unsuitable or missing and vice versa. In the end, 64% of the compensation sites had an unsuitable management relative to the defined compensation goals. 

Implementation instructions were not suitable relative to the defined compensation goals for 42% of the compensation sites. This was often the case for compensation plans related to the creation or restoration of meadows, pastures, or wetlands (21 of 31 sites, 68%). Plans often recommended unsuitable commercially available seed mixtures (10 of 31 sites, 32%) or failed to give specific instructions for the depletion of soil nutrient status, necessary rewetting measures, or methods for supplementary introduction or facilitation of target species (11 of 31 sites, 36%). Shortcomings in implementation instructions were also often found for measures intended for amphibians or dragonflies by creating ponds or revitalizing brooks (6 of 11 sites, 55%). As a rule, all deficiencies in implementation instructions were manifested in the phase of the implementation of the measure. Thus, unprofessional planning caused most of the implementation deficiencies. In addition, we found further minor deficiencies resulting from partial or faulty installation of compensation measures during this phase.

We examined 119 compensation sites in the selected 31 compensation areas and analyzed their success with respect to 326 defined compensation goals (Fig. 1a & 1b). Only 33% of the 326 defined compensation goals were fully or mostly achieved; 67% were reached only partly, mostly not, or not at all.

permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning: The lack of publicly available information about changes in the condition of offset sites was a major barrier to evaluation, given that condition is a key determinant of a site's habitat hectares score. While offset owners do have to submit annual monitoring reports to DELWP, these do not require field assessments to assess how the condition score of site changes over time. We therefore have little information about how responsive condition scores are to change, even under idealised offset management, which means it is challenging to conclusively know whether offset management is improving the condition of sites. Many of these barriers remain in today's reformed offsetting policy, with a recent Auditor General's report making numerous recommendations for how to improve the implementation, monitoring, data management and compliance surrounding Victoria's offsetting policy. Wildfires have the potential to bias our results if by chance they impact either offsets or controls differently, as woody vegetation can recover quickly after fire in a way which is not attributable to the treatment (offset management). This is especially of concern in Victoria during our evaluation period, as the Black Saturday fires in 2009 burned approximately 450,000 ha of bushland. To identify offsets and controls potentially impacted by wildfires, we visually assessed the time-series woody vegetation cover data for each land parcel for unusual reductions in woodland cover, then cross-referenced the parcel location against spatial data on all bushfires in Victoria occurring from 2008 to 2018 as assembled in Ward et al. (2019). We identified one land holding containing 12 early offsets which burned completely during the Black Saturday fires

permanence_risk_evidence_and_reasoning:
The permanence of offsets means that biodiversity gains are lasting and will continue to accrue through time. Appropriate legal warranties and funds are necessary to ensure permanence, as is risk management, monitoring, and independent auditing of outcomes (Maseyk et al., 2021; Quétier et al., 2014). Projects that require biodiversity offsets may have a long life cycle. This time allows the developer to build financial and institutional capacity to ensure permanence and to track the trajectory of restoration sites and adapt management as needed (Neri & Sánchez, 2010). As with other long-term plans, ongoing adaptive management measures must be in place to ensure long-term biodiversity outcomes can be achieved (Pope et al., 2021). Monitoring is necessary to determine whether gains are equivalent in quality and quantity compared with losses and to determine whether gains are attributable to the offset actions and would not have happened otherwise (Marshall et al., 2020; Sonter et al., 2020). An important issue in terms of permanence is the financial capacity of the offsets’ custodians to maintain the areas protected. Studying offsets of mining projects in Brazil, Souza and Sánchez (2018) found highly variable costs of up to 3.04% of the total investment for greenfield projects. An emerging concern is the risk associated with climate change. The implementation and maintenance of protected areas is still the cornerstone of conservation actions (Diniz et al., 2021). However, due to broad-scale environmental changes that could shift the distribution of habitats for many species, concern has been expressed that existing networks of protected areas might not be able to guarantee long-term persistence of the species they are supposed to protect (Lapola et al., 2020; Scarano & Ceotto, 2015).

Access control measures to the offset areas and maintenance of restoration areas were implemented and included in the respective management plans. The company maintains a team to inspect the areas, to prevent people and cattle invasions, and a fire brigade. According to 1 interviewee, these items represent the main threats and challenges to ensure conservation of the offset areas. They also represent permanent costs that need to be accounted for. Our document review and interviews revealed challenges for ensuring the permanence of the offset areas. Although the company has a program of long-term maintenance of offsets, these measures were not budgeted for in the Mine Closure Plan, which raises the question of whether there are sufficient and secure funds. The mine closure plan contains financial provision for expenses incurred after mine closure, but costs related to the maintenance of offset areas are not explicitly considered.
